I spend a lot of time in git. These aliases save me keystrokes every single day. Add them to your

[alias]
  co = checkout
  sw = switch
  br = branch
  st = status -sb
  lg = log --oneline --graph --decorate -20
  amend = commit --amend --no-edit
  wip = !git add -A && git commit -m "wip"
  undo = reset HEAD~1 --mixed
Shorthand for checkout and switch. I use

git sw feature-branch
git co -- src/file.ts
Short status with branch info. Compact and readable:

$ git st
## main...origin/main
 M src/app.ts
?? src/new-file.ts
A compact, visual log. Shows the last 20 commits with a graph:

$ git lg
* a1b2c3d (HEAD -> main) fix: resolve login bug
* d4e5f6a feat: add user dashboard
* g7h8i9j refactor: extract auth module
Add staged changes to the last commit without changing the message. Perfect for "oops, forgot a file":

JavaScript bundlers are one of those tools we use every day but rarely think about. Yet the history of bundlers tells the story of how frontend development itself has evolved — from simple script concatenation to sophisticated build pipelines.
The Early Days: Why We Needed Bundlers
Before bundlers, we loaded JavaScript with multiple
— you manually tracked what needed what
— each file was a separate HTTP request
calls, bundle everything into a single file, and wrap modules in closures. Simple, revolutionary, but limited.
Event emitters are everywhere — in Node.js, in the DOM, in frontend frameworks. But most implementations are loosely typed. You can emit an event with the wrong payload and TypeScript won't catch it.
Let's fix that by building a fully type-safe event emitter from scratch.
We want an event emitter where:
Event names are constrained to a known set
Each event has a specific payload type
Emitting an event with the wrong payload is a compile error
We start with a generic interface that maps event names to their payload types:

type EventMap = Record<string, any>;

type EventHandler<T = any> = (payload: T) => void;
will be the type parameter users provide — an object where keys are event names and values are payload types.
Unnecessary re-renders are the most common performance issue in React apps. Here's how to find and fix them.
The Profiler tab in React DevTools is the best tool for this:
Open React DevTools, go to the
The flame graph shows which components rendered and why
Look for components that render frequently but don't visually change — those are your targets.
This adds a visual flash around components as they re-render. If you see the entire page flashing on every keystroke, you have a problem.

TypeScript 4.1 introduced template literal types, bringing string manipulation to the type level. They're more useful than you might think.
Template literal types work like JavaScript template literals, but for types:

type Greeting = `hello ${string}`;

const a: Greeting = "hello world";  // OK
const b: Greeting = "hello";        // OK
const c: Greeting = "goodbye world"; // Error
TypeScript provides utility types for transforming string types:

type Upper = Uppercase<"hello">;      // "HELLO"
type Lower = Lowercase<"HELLO">;      // "hello"
type Cap = Capitalize<"hello">;       // "Hello"
type Uncap = Uncapitalize<"Hello">;   // "hello"

CSS specificity determines which styles win when multiple rules target the same element. Understanding it saves hours of debugging.
Specificity is calculated as a three-part score:
Higher specificity always wins. Within the same specificity, the last rule wins.

Keep elements that pass a test:

Check if any or all elements match:

const hasAdmin = users.some((user) => user.role === "admin"); // true/false
const allAdults = users.every((user) => user.age >= 18);      // true/false

I used Yarn for years. Yarn 1 was a great improvement over early npm, and Yarn Berry introduced interesting ideas with Plug'n'Play. But after trying pnpm on a few projects, I switched entirely. Here's why.
. Instead of copying packages into each project's
, it hard-links them from a global store. If ten projects use the same version of React, it's stored once on disk.
On my machine, this saved over 20GB across all my projects.
Because pnpm only needs to link files rather than copy them, installs are significantly faster — especially on CI where the global store can be cached:

# Typical CI install times (medium project)
yarn install: ~45s
pnpm install: ~20s
structure. Your code can only import packages that are explicitly listed in your
. This catches phantom dependencies — packages you accidentally rely on because they're hoisted by another dependency.
pnpm has built-in workspace support that just works:
Filtering, running scripts across packages, and managing shared dependencies all work out of the box. No need for additional tools like Lerna.

Good design isn't just about aesthetics—it's about creating interfaces that are intuitive, accessible, and delightful to use. Here are the core principles that guide my approach to web interface design.
The primary goal of any interface is to communicate clearly. Users should never have to guess what an element does or where they should click next.
Use descriptive labels instead of clever metaphors
Maintain consistent terminology throughout the interface
Provide clear visual hierarchy with typography and spacing
Show system status and provide feedback for user actions
Don't overwhelm users with every option at once. Reveal complexity gradually as users need it.
Good interfaces work like a conversation—they reveal information at the right pace, neither too much nor too little.
Start with the most common use cases
Use expandable sections for advanced options
Provide defaults that work for most users
Jekyll is a static site generator that transforms plain text into static websites. Combined with GitHub Pages, it provides a powerful, free hosting solution for blogs and documentation sites.
No database or server-side code to manage
Free hosting with custom domain support
Fast page loads and excellent security

by Andrew Hunt and David Thomas is one of those rare technical books that remains relevant decades after publication. Updated in 2019 for its 20th anniversary, this book distills practical wisdom for software engineers at any career stage.
Craftsmanship and professionalism in software development
Practical techniques for writing better code
"Care about your craft. Think! About your work."
This opening sets the tone for the entire book—programming is a craft that demands thoughtfulness and continuous improvement.

One of the most powerful concepts in the book: don't leave "broken windows" (bad code, poor decisions) unrepaired. Once you allow one broken window, more will follow, and the entire project will deteriorate.
When you encounter bad code, fix it immediately or file a ticket. Don't let it become normalized.

Social networks have fundamentally transformed how we communicate, form relationships, and organize society. This exploration examines the structural properties of networks and their profound impact on modern life.
At their core, social networks are patterns of relationships between individuals, groups, or organizations. These connections can represent:
Social networks aren't new—humans have always formed networks. What's changed is:
Digital platforms enable networks of billions
Network structures are now observable and analyzable
We can consciously design and manipulate networks
